meta.title: Unité 2 — Habitat, Verbs & Prepositions with Countries
meta.topic: Housing vocabulary, prepositions with countries and contracted articles, new verbs (regarder, lire, dire, connaître, faire, aimer), object pronouns, and deeper grammar including gender/number agreement and il y a.

vocabulary.notes: Feminine. Most common word for home.
Feminine. Usually a large detached house.
Masculine. Typically found outside city centres.
Masculine. Also called 'la salle de séjour'.
Feminine. Toilet is often in a separate room in France.
Always plural. 'Où sont les toilettes ?' = Where is the toilet?
Masculine. 'Son sac' = his/her bag. From class chat.
Invariable expression. 'Il y a un lit.' = There is a bed. 'Il y a des livres.' = There are some books.
Adverb. 'Il y a toujours un stylo dans mon sac.' = There is always a pen in my bag.
Adverb. Placed after the verb: 'Je parle aussi anglais.' = I also speak English.
Used to sequence actions: 'Je mange, puis je dors.' = I eat, then I sleep.
Relative pronoun or question word. 'Qu'est-ce que' = what is. Before a vowel: qu'.
Common question structure. 'Qu'est-ce que c'est ?' = What is it? 'Qu'est-ce que tu fais ?' = What are you doing?
Both an indefinite article (des livres = some books) and a contracted article (de + les = des). Context determines meaning.
Same form for masculine and feminine adjective.
Adjective. 'un autre livre' = another book. Plural: d'autres.
Same form for masculine and feminine. 'Elle est très célèbre.' = She is very famous.
Adverb. Always placed before the adjective it modifies.
Conjunction. Never pronounced with a final t sound.
IMPORTANT: 'et' (and) vs 'est' (is) — same sound, different spelling. 'Il est grand et fort.' = He is tall and strong.
Regular -ER verb. 'Il chante.' = He sings. From class chat (Mika reference).
From class chat. Masculine: anglais. Feminine: anglaise. Note: nationality adjectives are lowercase in French.
Masculine: joli. Feminine: jolie. 'Un joli appartement.' = A nice apartment.
Same form for masculine and feminine.
Same form for masculine and feminine.

vocabulary.french: un joueur / une joueuse de tennis

verbs.notes: Regular -ER verb. Common mistake spotted in class: 'regadrez' is WRONG — correct form is 'regardez'. The final -ent in ils/elles is always silent.
examples: Je regarde la télévision. — I watch television.
Regardez ! — Look! (command, formal/plural)
Elle regarde par la fenêtre. — She looks out the window.

verbs.notes: Irregular verb. Je and tu are identical (lis). Note the silent -t in il/elle lit.
examples: Je lis un livre. — I am reading a book.
Tu lis le journal ? — Are you reading the newspaper?
Ils lisent beaucoup. — They read a lot.

verbs.notes: Irregular verb. KEY EXCEPTION: vous dites (NOT 'disez'). One of only 3 irregular vous forms: dire→dites, faire→faites, être→êtes.
examples: Je dis la vérité. — I am telling the truth.
Comment dit-on 'hello' en français ? — How do you say 'hello' in French?
Qu'est-ce que tu dis ? — What are you saying?

verbs.notes: Irregular. Note circumflex on connaît. CRITICAL distinction: connaître = to know a person/place (familiarity). savoir = to know a fact/how to do something. Direct object pronoun: 'Je les connais' (I know them) — pronoun goes BEFORE the verb.
examples: Oui, je les connais. — Yes, I know them.
Non, je ne les connais pas. — No, I don't know them.
Vous connaissez Paris ? — Do you know Paris?

verbs.notes: Highly irregular. KEY EXCEPTION: vous faites (NOT 'faisez'). 'Qu'est-ce que tu fais ?' = What are you doing? Also used in weather: 'Il fait chaud' = It is hot.
examples: Qu'est-ce que tu fais ? — What are you doing?
Je fais du sport. — I do sport.
Il fait beau aujourd'hui. — The weather is nice today.

verbs.notes: Regular -ER verb. Followed by infinitive: 'J'aime chanter.' = I like to sing. Followed by noun: 'J'aime la musique.' = I like music. Note: with infinitives, no article is used.
examples: J'aime la musique et les jeux vidéo. — I like music and video games.
Elle aime chanter. — She likes to sing.
Vous aimez le français ? — Do you like French?
Fermez la porte, s'il vous plaît. — Close the door, please.
Je ferme la fenêtre. — I close the window.
Il me montre la photo. — He shows me the photo.
Montrez-moi votre passeport. — Show me your passport.

verbs.notes: Reflexive -ER verb with stem change: inquié- → inquiè- in je/tu/il/ils forms. 'Ne t'inquiète pas !' = Don't worry!
examples: Ne t'inquiète pas ! — Don't worry!
Je m'inquiète pour toi. — I worry about you.

verbs.notes: Irregular. 'Je viens de' + country = I come from. Use de + l' before vowel: 'Je viens de l'Inde.' Use du for masculine countries: 'Je viens du Japon.' Use de for countries without article (rare).
examples: Je viens de l'Inde. — I come from India.
Je viens du Japon. — I come from Japan.
Je viens du Canada. — I come from Canada.
Je viens du Bénin. — I come from Benin.

verbs.notes: Irregular. 'Je vais au Canada.' = I am going to Canada (au = à + le, masculine country). 'Je vais à Ludhiana.' = I am going to Ludhiana (city, no article). Also used for near future: 'Je vais manger.' = I am going to eat.
examples: Je vais au Canada. — I am going to Canada.
Je vais à Ludhiana. — I am going to Ludhiana.
Elle est très célèbre. — She is very famous.
Je vais manger. — I am going to eat.

grammar.title: Contracted articles (Articles contractés) — au, aux, du, des
grammar.explanation: When the prepositions à or de combine with the definite articles le or les, they must contract. à + le = au (never 'à le'), à + les = aux (never 'à les'), de + le = du (never 'de le'), de + les = des (never 'de les'). There is NO contraction with la or l': à + la = à la, à + l' = à l', de + la = de la, de + l' = de l'. Examples: Je vais au Canada (à + le Canada). Je viens du Japon (de + le Japon). Je parle aux étudiants (à + les étudiants).

grammar.title: Prepositions with countries — en / au / aux / à + de / du / de l'
grammar.explanation: To say 'in/to' a country: en + feminine country (en France, en Inde, en Italie — most countries ending in -e are feminine). au + masculine country (au Japon, au Canada, au Maroc). aux + plural country (aux États-Unis). à + city (à Paris, à Delhi, à Ludhiana). EXCEPTIONS — masculine despite ending in -e: le Mexique, le Mozambique, le Zaïre, le Zimbabwe. To say 'from': je viens de + feminine/vowel (je viens de France, je viens de l'Inde). je viens du + masculine (je viens du Japon, je viens du Canada). Class drill result: 'Je viens de l'Inde' is correct — NOT 'je viens du Inde' (Inde is feminine).

grammar.title: il y a — there is / there are
grammar.explanation: 'Il y a' is an invariable expression meaning 'there is' or 'there are' — it never changes form. il y a + singular: Il y a un livre. (There is a book.) il y a + plural: Il y a des livres. (There are some books.) Negative: Il n'y a pas de livre. (There is no book.) Note: after negation, des/un/une become 'de/d'': Il n'y a pas de livres. Questions: Est-ce qu'il y a un café ? (Is there a coffee shop?) / Il y a un café ? (informal)

grammar.title: Gender and number agreement of adjectives
grammar.explanation: Adjectives in French must agree with the noun they describe in gender (masculine/feminine) and number (singular/plural). Basic rules: add -e for feminine (joli → jolie, français → française), add -s for plural (joli → jolis), add -es for feminine plural (jolie → jolies). Some adjectives are invariable (same for m/f): magnifique, pratique, célèbre, calme. Some have irregular feminines: breton → bretonne (double consonant), italien → italienne (double n). Masculine adjectives ending in -e don't change in feminine: magnifique → magnifique.

grammar.title: Masculine vs feminine plurals — special cases
grammar.explanation: Most nouns and adjectives add -s in the plural. Special cases: words ending in -eau, -eu, -au add -x instead of -s (un château → des châteaux, un bureau → des bureaux). Words already ending in -s, -x, or -z don't change (un bras → des bras). Irregular: un œil → des yeux (eyes). These rules apply to both nouns and adjectives.

grammar.title: Direct object pronouns — le, la, l', les
grammar.explanation: Object pronouns replace a noun that is the direct object of the verb. They go BEFORE the conjugated verb. le = him/it (masculine singular), la = her/it (feminine singular), l' = him/her before a vowel or h, les = them (plural). Word order: Subject + [ne] + pronoun + verb + [pas]. Examples: Je connais Marie → Je la connais. Je connais les acteurs → Je les connais. Negative: Je ne les connais pas. CLASS DRILL — common errors: ✗ 'je ne pas les connais' ✗ 'je ne connais les pas' ✓ 'je ne les connais pas'.

grammar.title: et vs est — same sound, different meaning
grammar.explanation: From class chat: 'et' (and) and 'est' (is, from être) are pronounced identically — both sound like 'ay'. They must never be confused in writing. 'et' is a conjunction linking two things: 'Il est grand et fort.' 'est' is the 3rd person singular of être: 'Il est chanteur.' Memory tip: 'et' ends in -t (never pronounced) and comes between two items. 'est' comes directly after a subject pronoun or noun and is followed by an adjective, noun, or adverb.

grammar.title: Asking questions with est-ce que
grammar.explanation: 'Est-ce que' (literally 'is it that') is the standard way to turn any statement into a yes/no question without changing word order. Statement: Tu parles français. Question: Est-ce que tu parles français ? Before a vowel: 'Est-ce qu'il parle français ?' No inversion needed — just add est-ce que at the start. 'Qu'est-ce que' = 'what' in questions: Qu'est-ce que c'est ? (What is it?), Qu'est-ce que tu fais ? (What are you doing?).

grammar.title: comment s'appelle-t-il/elle — asking someone's name in 3rd person
grammar.explanation: To ask about a third person's name: 'Comment s'appelle-t-il ?' (What is his name?) or 'Comment s'appelle-t-elle ?' (What is her name?). The -t- between s'appelle and il/elle is a purely phonetic liaison inserted to avoid two vowel sounds in a row — it has no grammatical meaning. Answer: 'Il s'appelle Mika.' / 'Elle s'appelle Kristin.' From class chat: 'comment s'appelle-t-elle ?' was used when discussing the actress.

grammar.title: Prepositions of place (Prépositions de lieu)
grammar.explanation: sur (on), sous (under), dans (in/inside), devant (in front of), derrière (behind), entre (between), à côté de (next to), près de (near/nearby), à (at/to/in). New from Day 3: 'dans' vs 'en' — both can mean 'in' but 'dans' implies physically inside something (dans le sac = in the bag, dans l'appartement = in the apartment), while 'en' is used with countries and certain time expressions.

grammar.title: Possessive adjectives — special rule before vowels
grammar.explanation: The possessive adjectives agree with the noun they describe, not the owner. mon/ma/mes (my), ton/ta/tes (your), son/sa/ses (his/her). IMPORTANT RULE: Before a feminine noun beginning with a vowel or silent h, use mon/ton/son instead of ma/ta/sa to avoid a vowel clash: mon amie (not 'ma amie'), ton adresse (not 'ta adresse'), son appartement (not 'sa appartement'). This applies to: adresse, amie, école, and any feminine noun starting with a vowel.

key_phrases.french: Il y a un lit dans la chambre.
key_phrases.english: There is a bed in the bedroom.
key_phrases.context: Using il y a to describe a room

key_phrases.french: Qu'est-ce qu'il y a dans ton sac ?
key_phrases.context: Asking about contents — combines qu'est-ce que + il y a

key_phrases.french: J'associe le numéro 1 avec la fiche C.
key_phrases.english: I associate number 1 with card C.

key_phrases.context: Correct form — de + l' before vowel. NOT 'du Inde'.
au = à + le (masculine country). NOT 'je vais a Canada'.
Cities take à — no article, no contraction needed.
Object pronoun before verb — correct word order

key_phrases.french: Non, je ne les connais pas.
key_phrases.context: Negation with object pronoun: ne + les + verb + pas
The -t- is a phonetic liaison, not a grammatical marker

key_phrases.english: I live in a small apartment.

key_phrases.french: Il y a toujours un stylo dans mon sac.
key_phrases.english: There is always a pen in my bag.
key_phrases.context: Using il y a with toujours

cultural_notes.title: La France — Géographie
cultural_notes.content: France is the largest country in the European Union by area. It has 27 regions, 7 mountain ranges (massifs), 5 overseas territories (DOM-TOM: Guadeloupe, Martinique, Guyane, La Réunion, Mayotte), and 10 national parks. Its varied landscape — Alps, Pyrenees, Atlantic coast, Mediterranean coast — makes it one of the most geographically diverse countries in Europe.

cultural_notes.title: Marseille — Capitale Culturelle 2013
cultural_notes.content: Marseille was the European Capital of Culture in 2013. Key landmarks: MuCEM (Musée des Civilisations de l'Europe et de la Méditerranée — Museum of Mediterranean Civilisations), Vieux-Port (Old Port, the heart of the city), Notre-Dame de la Garde (nicknamed 'La Bonne Mère', the protector of sailors), and the Château d'If (famous from Alexandre Dumas' The Count of Monte Cristo).

cultural_notes.title: Mika — French pop icon
cultural_notes.content: Mika (full name: Michael Holbrook Penniman Jr.) is a Lebanese-British singer who became hugely popular in France and throughout the French-speaking world. He is known for songs like 'Grace Kelly' and 'Relax (Take It Easy)'. He was a coach on the French TV show 'The Voice' for many years, which made him a household name in France. He is an example of a 'chanteur' — a male singer.

cultural_notes.title: La colocation en France
cultural_notes.content: Flat/house sharing (la colocation, or 'la coloc' informally) is extremely common in France, especially in big cities like Paris where rents are very high. Key colocation vocabulary: respecter l'espace (respect personal space), payer les factures (pay bills), organiser le ménage (organise cleaning). The verb 'partager' (to share) is also very useful: 'Je partage un appartement.' = I share an apartment.

cultural_notes.title: Kristin Scott Thomas — British actress in France
cultural_notes.content: Kristin Scott Thomas (born 1960) is a British actress famous for her work in both English and French cinema. She is equally fluent in both languages and has starred in iconic French films. She is a perfect example to illustrate: un acteur (male actor) / une actrice (female actress), the use of 'quel/quelle' (Quel est son prénom ?), and how to say a birth year (Elle est née en mille neuf cent soixante).

practice_exercises.instruction: Fill in the correct contracted article or preposition: au / aux / du / de la / de l' / en / à

items.prompt: Je viens ___ Inde. (from India — feminine, starts with vowel)
Je vais ___ Canada. (to Canada — masculine country)
Elle vient ___ États-Unis. (from the USA — plural)
Il habite ___ Paris. (in Paris — city)
Je viens ___ France. (from France — feminine country)
Il vient ___ Japon. (from Japan — masculine country)

practice_exercises.instruction: Correct the word order — rewrite using the correct position of the object pronoun

items.prompt: Non, je ne pas les connais. → ___
items.answer: Non, je ne les connais pas.

items.prompt: Non, je ne connais les pas. → ___
items.answer: Non, je ne les connais pas.

items.prompt: Oui, je connais les. → ___
Non, je ne le connais pas. (him) → Is this correct?
items.answer: Yes — 'je ne le connais pas' is correct for a single masculine person/thing.

items.prompt: There is a washing machine in the kitchen.
items.answer: Il y a un lave-linge dans la cuisine.

items.prompt: I am going to Canada.
She comes from India.
What are you doing?
I don't know them.
She was born in 1960.
items.answer: Elle est née en mille neuf cent soixante.

items.prompt: Il ___ chanteur ___ acteur.
Elle ___ très célèbre.
La maison ___ grande ___ belle.
Mika ___ un chanteur célèbre.
Vous ___ la porte. (dire) — dis / dites / disez
Vous ___ du sport. (faire) — faisez / faites / font
Ils ___ le journal. (lire) — lisent / lirent / lissent
Je ___ Paris très bien. (connaître) — connais / connaît / connaise

extra_sections.label: 🏠 Describing Your Home — Speaking Template
extra_sections.content:
Use this structure for the speaking task 'Décrivez votre maison':

J'habite dans [un appartement / une maison / un loft].
Il y a [nombre] pièces.
Il y a [une cuisine / un salon / une chambre / une salle de bains].
Dans ma chambre, il y a un lit et une table.
J'aime mon appartement parce qu'il est [pratique / joli / grand / confortable].

Example: J'habite dans un appartement. Il y a trois pièces. Il y a une cuisine, un salon et une chambre. Dans mon salon, il y a une table et des chaises. J'aime mon appartement parce qu'il est pratique.

extra_sections.label: ✍️ Class Correction Drill — Je viens de…
extra_sections.content: The class worked through the correct form of 'Je viens de' with different countries.
items: ❌ Je viens du Inde — WRONG: Inde is feminine, no contraction needed
❌ je viens du inde — WRONG: same error + lowercase
✅ Je viens de l'Inde — CORRECT: de + l' before vowel
✅ Je viens du Bénin — CORRECT: Bénin is masculine → du
✅ Je viens du Canada — CORRECT: Canada is masculine → du
Rule: de + l' (vowel), de + la (feminine consonant), du (masculine), des (plural)
Most countries ending in -e are feminine: France, Inde, Italie, Chine, Espagne, Allemagne
Exceptions (masculine despite -e): Mexique, Mozambique, Zaïre, Zimbabwe

extra_sections.label: ✍️ Class Correction Drill — Je vais au / à…
extra_sections.content: The class also drilled going TO places.
items: ❌ Je vais du canada — WRONG: 'du' is for 'from', not 'to'
❌ Je Vais a Canada — WRONG: masculine country needs au, not bare à
✅ Je vais au Canada — CORRECT: à + le Canada = au
✅ je vais à Ludhiana — CORRECT: cities take à (no article)
Rule: going TO → au (masc.), en (fem.), aux (plural), à (city)
Rule: coming FROM → du (masc.), de/de la (fem.), des (plural), de (city)

Write 5 sentences using possessive adjectives (mon/ma/mes, ton/ta/tes, son/sa/ses)
Write 3 sentences using ce/cette/cet/ces

extra_sections.label: 🔑 Key Vocabulary — Dans le sac (In the bag)
items: un passeport — a passport
un rouge à lèvres — a lipstick
une carte d'identité — an ID card
un portable — a mobile phone
un carnet / un cahier — a notebook
un stylo — a pen
une trousse — a pencil case / pouch
des clés — keys
des mouchoirs — tissues
un parapluie — an umbrella
Template sentence: J'ai ___ dans mon sac. = I have ___ in my bag.

extra_sections.label: 🌍 Countries — Feminine/Masculine Quick Reference
items: FEMININE (en / de): la France, l'Inde, l'Italie, la Chine, l'Espagne, l'Allemagne, la Russie, l'Australie
MASCULINE (au / du): le Japon, le Canada, le Maroc, le Brésil, le Mexique, le Bénin
PLURAL (aux / des): les États-Unis, les Pays-Bas
EXCEPTIONS — end in -e but MASCULINE: le Mexique, le Mozambique, le Zaïre, le Zimbabwe
RULE: If a country starts with a vowel, use en (not au) even if masculine: en Iran, en Irak, en Ouganda
